user_prompt: I need a python script, `tools\package_web_assets.py`, which will be run as a part of the build process before compilation, edit `platformio.ini` to include it
pay attention to `inc\defs.h` the struct `web_asset_desc_t`, we will need this
use argparse, take these optional args
* input directory, default is the `html` directory in this project
the script needs to import libraries for minifying html, css, javascript, and also gzip compress

scan through the directory, handle all files, any unknown file types are considered octet stream. Compress the data with gzip and create a byte array for it. Create a `web_asset_desc_t` entry for it. The final header file might looks something like this
the `minify` argument enables minification of `html`, `css`, and `js` files.
only process `css` files if `include_css` is enabled
only process `js` files if `include_js` is enabled

if `html_sub` is true and a HTML file is being processed, then look for `<link rel="stylesheet" href="path" />` and `<script src="path"></script>`, and find the file reference, and replace the linking tag with a tag that includes the referenced file inline.
When writing the binary blobs, use a format like `0xAB`, notice the capitalization, and also, line break every 16 bytes.
agent_reply: - Uses `argparse` with the requested defaults and flags.
- Skips standalone `.css` and `.js` unless `--include-css` / `--include-js` are set.
- Optionally inlines local stylesheet links and script `src` references with `--html-sub`.
- Emits `0xAB` byte formatting with 16 bytes per line.
- Writes `web_asset_desc_t` entries and avoids rewriting the header if unchanged.
I wired it into [platformio.ini](/D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/platformio.ini) as a pre-build script:
That is the PlatformIO hook intended for scripts that run before the build.
I also changed [defs.h](/D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/defs.h) so `web_asset_desc_t::ptr_payload` is `const uint8_t*`, matching the generated immutable byte arrays.

The default run generated [web_assets.h](/D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/web_assets.h). With the requested defaults it currently includes `index.html` only; `styles.css` is skipped unless CSS inclusion or HTML substitution is enabled.
The PlatformIO build succeeded and the pre-build asset packer ran during it.

user_prompt: add a web server handler for "/reset_password", and the javascript function `passreset_onsubmit` will be the one to submit information to it via ajax
under security-level 0, "/reset_password" replies with an appropriate code indicating that it is not implemented or something to that effect.
* the frontend takes the password and generates filecrypt-key and network-key using `network_salt` and `filecrypt_salt` (this seems missing, copy it from `kSaltFilecrypt`)
* the backend receives these and saves them to the NVS through `Aegis`
* the frontend takes the password and generates network-key using `network_salt`
* the backend receives it and saves it to the NVS through `Aegis`, filecrypt-key is regenerated randomly

also, while you are changing this part, the password entry field needs a second duplicate password entry for the user to confirm the password by typing it twice. Disallow the change if they don't match. Make it look nice, so instead of everything in one row, now there's like... three rows of elements? Use a hidden 4th row to pop up the error that the passwords don't match (make it handle other errors, we might add other validations).
agent_reply: - Added Aegis helpers to save both keys together, or save network key while regenerating filecrypt key randomly in [Aegis.h](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/Security/Aegis.h) and [Aegis.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/Security/Aegis.cpp).
- Added frontend PBKDF2 derivation for `network_key` and `filecrypt_key` using `network_salt` and `filecrypt_salt` in [script.js](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/html/script.js).
- Added `passreset_onsubmit()` with password confirmation, mismatch/error display, AJAX submit, and session refresh.

- Reworked the password reset UI into a cleaner multi-row form with a hidden message row in [index.html](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/html/index.html) and [styles.css](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/html/styles.css).
- Security level `0`: endpoint returns `501 Not Implemented`.
- Security level `1`: frontend derives and sends both `network_key` and `filecrypt_key`; backend saves both through `Aegis`.

user_prompt: ### Task: pin code related web server changes and front-end changes

The `/get_cfg` handler, specifically inside `bool build_cfg_json(String& json)`, will add a key `pincode` that exposes the raw pincode stored by `pin_code_cfg_t` but only if it is valid (active, not an empty string), otherwise it will reply with a string indicating if it was “not set” or “revoked on <date>”, depending on the other members of `pin_code_cfg_t`

The front-end will append this as the last item of the information panel, but put it in a textbox that the user can choose to hide and unhide from view, I forgot if this is possible with a standard `password` type input.
(the above functionality only applies for security level 1 and above)
`/set_cfg` does not need to worry about pin code at all.
`index.html` already has a panel for “Pin Code Reset”
on page load, hide `#pincode_reset_panel` first, its usage depends on authentication

when `/get_cfg`arrives to the front-end, depending on security level, configure this panel to show the correct UI, because security level 1 allows for customizable pin while level 2 and above uses a generated pin. If the security level is 0, then hide this panel completely as pin codes are not used.

oh and this panel is also following the same rule as the password reset, it must be done on a secure default soft AP connection, otherwise, do the same thing that is done to the password reset panel (I forgot if I hide it or make it say a message)

The backend `lib\WifiManager\WebCfgHandlers.cpp` needs to implement both `/set_custom_pin` and `/reset_pin_code`. This should just wire into `lib\PinCode\PinCode.cpp`, but both functions need to authenticate in the exact same way `/set_cfg` checks for authentication

implement `pincodereset_onsubmit` for both security level thresholds. if the pin is custom, validate it first, notify the user if it doesn’t meet the rules or isn’t matching between the two fields. Then, depending on the security level, use either `/set_custom_pin` or `/reset_pin_code` while also remembering to use the same authentication `/set_cfg` uses
`/set_custom_pin` and `/reset_pin_code` both reply in the exact same way that `/get_cfg` replies, exactly. the front-end javascript handles the reply in the exact same way
the existence of `/set_custom_pin` depends on `BUILD_WITH_SECURITY_LEVEL` being == 1
agent_reply: - Added encrypted `/set_custom_pin` for security level 1 only.
- Both PIN POST endpoints reuse the `/set_cfg` encrypted-body auth path and return the same encrypted config reply shape as `/get_cfg`.
- Frontend now hides the PIN panel on load, configures it from `/get_cfg`, shows custom vs generated mode by security level, and appends PIN info as a password-style textbox with show/hide.
- PIN reset UI follows the generated soft AP rule, and the backend enforces that too.
Note: `src/version.c` changed as a normal build-generated side effect.

user_prompt: there is a delete button for files listed in the front-end ui, explain how the graphic icon is implemented
agent_reply: The file delete icon is pure CSS, not an image asset.
In [html/index.html](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/html/index.html:44), each file row has an empty anchor:
Then [html/script.js](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/html/script.js:159) clones the row template for each file. For the delete anchor, it only sets:
The graphic itself is drawn in [html/styles.css](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/html/styles.css:209):
- `.delete-link` creates a 34x34 square button with a dark red background and pink `color`.
- `.delete-link::before` draws the trash can body using a positioned rectangle border.
- `.delete-link::after` draws the lid using a small horizontal rectangle.
- The little handle is faked with `box-shadow: 3px -3px 0 -1px currentColor`.
- `currentColor` means the pseudo-elements inherit the anchor’s `color`, so hover can recolor the whole icon just by changing `.delete-link:hover { color: ... }`.
So the “icon” is a handcrafted CSS trash can made from two pseudo-elements, not SVG, PNG, font icon, or lucide.
The file delete icon is pure CSS, not an image asset.
In [html/index.html](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/html/index.html:44), each file row has an empty anchor:
Then [html/script.js](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/html/script.js:159) clones the row template for each file. For the delete anchor, it only sets:
The graphic itself is drawn in [html/styles.css](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/html/styles.css:209):
- `.delete-link` creates a 34x34 square button with a dark red background and pink `color`.
- `.delete-link::before` draws the trash can body using a positioned rectangle border.
- `.delete-link::after` draws the lid using a small horizontal rectangle.
- The little handle is faked with `box-shadow: 3px -3px 0 -1px currentColor`.
- `currentColor` means the pseudo-elements inherit the anchor’s `color`, so hover can recolor the whole icon just by changing `.delete-link:hover { color: ... }`.
So the “icon” is a handcrafted CSS trash can made from two pseudo-elements, not SVG, PNG, font icon, or lucide.
